title: Lower GI Bleed
oneLiner: Bleeding from colon/rectum/anus, often self-limiting but can cause life-threatening shock in elderly/anticoagulated patients.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Haemorrhagic shock (massive diverticular/angiodysplasia bleed):** A patient with painless, large-volume bright red blood per rectum + tachycardia (HR >100) and hypotension (SBP <90) – do not assume it is trivial.
- **Upper GI bleed presenting as fresh PR blood:** If the patient is shocked but has no melaena/haematemesis, always insert an NG tube (or consider OGD before colonoscopy) – a brisk upper GI bleed can cause bright red PR blood.
- **Mesenteric ischaemia:** Severe abdominal pain *out of proportion* to exam + bloody diarrhoea – lactate >2.5, peritonism – requires urgent CT angiogram / surgical review.
- **Perforated diverticulitis:** Lower quadrant tenderness, guarding, rigid abdomen on exam – do not attribute bleeding to simple diverticulosis; perforation requires emergency surgery.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Bright red blood per rectum (haematochezia), maroon stools, clots, or blood mixed with stool. Tenesmus (rectal urgency) suggests distal source. Nausea/syncope if significant blood loss.
- **Key exam findings:** Pallor, tachycardia, postural hypotension. Abdominal exam: check for localised tenderness (diverticulitis, IBD), guarding/peritonism (mesenteric ischaemia, perforation). PR exam: source (haemorrhoids, fissure, mass), stool colour (bright red vs melaena).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Upper GI bleed** – haematochezia if massive; melaena, NG aspirate positive for blood – differentiate with OGD.
- **Diverticular bleed** – painless, large volume, left-sided – usually self-limiting.
- **Angiodysplasia** – painless, right colon, older patient – may require CT angiography.
- **IBD (UC/Crohn’s)** – bloody diarrhoea, abdominal pain, tenesmus – stool culture before steroids.
- **Colorectal cancer** – chronic, small-volume bleeding, change in bowel habit, weight loss – colonoscopy needed.
- **Haemorrhoids/fissure** – bright red blood on toilet paper, after defaecation – PR exam often diagnostic.
- **Infectious colitis (e.g. Shigella, Campylobacter)** – fever, abdominal cramps, watery diarrhoea ± blood – stool MC&S.
- **Ischaemic colitis** – elderly, cardiovascular disease, acute onset pain, bloody diarrhoea – CT with contrast, do not scope acutely.

**Splitter:** Painless, large-volume bleeding + shock → diverticular/angiodysplasia (not colitis). If pain is present with diarrhoea → IBD or infectious colitis. Melaena/haematemesis → UGIB.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Full vital signs including lying/standing BP. PR exam – document source, stool colour, mass. Place IV access (widebore, ×2 if shocked). NG tube if UGIB suspected (but not always needed).
- **Bloods:** FBC (Hb, platelets), U+E, LFTs, coagulation screen (PT/APTT), group & screen +/- crossmatch if shock. Lactate (metabolic stress). Consider troponin in elderly.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** CT angiography if massive ongoing bleeding (active extravasation) or haemodynamic instability despite resuscitation.  
  - **Second-line:** Colonoscopy after bowel preparation if bleeding slows – also therapeutic (clipping, adrenaline injection, APC).  
- **Special tests:** Capsule endoscopy if colonoscopy negative and ongoing bleed suspected from small bowel.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **Oakland Score (for safe discharge):** Age >60 (1pt), HR >100 (1pt), SBP <100 (1pt), Hb <110 (1pt) – score <8 suggests safe for outpatient management.  
  - **Re-bleeding risk:** Blatchford score (commoner for UGIB but adapted) – not routinely used for LGIB.
- **Never-miss test:** **Coagulation screen (INR, APTT)** – this is the one test you cannot omit; if elevated, reverse immediately. Also **pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age (rules out ectopic/obstetric causes that can mimic).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Resuscitate: IV crystalloid (500–1000 mL stat), blood crossmatch if shock (if Hb <70 g/L, or <80 in cardiac disease).  
  - Reverse anticoagulation: Warfarin → IV vitamin K (10 mg slow IV) + PCC (dose per local policy – [check local policy] for INR-based dose). DOACs → consider idarucizumab (dabigatran) or andexanet alfa (rivaroxaban/apixaban) – use only if major/life-threatening bleed.  
  - If shocked, call senior for possible interventional radiology (embolisation) or urgent OGD to rule out UGIB.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue IV fluids, monitor haemoglobin (repeat fingerprick hourly if ongoing).  
  - Arrange **colonoscopy** (after bowel prep – polyethylene glycol) within 24–48h if stable.  
  - If ongoing bleeding and colonoscopy not possible, discuss with interventional radiology for angiography ± embolisation.  
  - Treat underlying cause: antibiotics if diverticulitis (co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS PO, or ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD + metronidazole 400 mg TDS if penicillin allergy – check local policy).  
  - Iron replacement: oral ferrous fumarate (1–2 tablets BD) if Hb stable but low.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - If haemorrhoids/fissure: high-fibre diet, laxatives (e.g. ispaghula husk), topical lidocaine/prudacaine.  
  - If diverticular disease: increase fibre, avoid NSAIDs.  
  - IBD: refer to gastroenterology for management of colitis.  
  - Cancer: urgent referral to colorectal team (2-week wait).  
  - Iron deficiency workup: repeat FBC at 6 weeks, consider ferritin, colonoscopy if not already done.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV Hartmann’s or 0.9% NaCl at 125 mL/h (adjust for shock).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (40 mg SC OD, or 20 mg if eGFR <30) – hold if actively bleeding, restart once haemostasis achieved.  
  - Diet: NBM if colonoscopy planned; otherwise light diet if stable.  
  - Observations: NEWS 4-hourly (if stable) or hourly if active bleeding.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Tranexamic acid – not routinely recommended for lower GI bleed (evidence lacking), but can consider in significant ongoing bleed (1 g IV over 10 min, then 1 g IV over 8h – but discuss with senior).  
  - Anticoagulation reversal:  
    - Warfarin: Vitamin K 5–10 mg slow IV (check local policy for dose weight-based).  
    - PCC (Beriplex/Octaplex): dose per INR – [check local policy] (e.g. 25–50 U/kg).  
  - Vasopressors: not routine – only if hypotensive despite adequate fluid + blood (discuss with ITU).
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - For diverticulitis: IV antibiotics – if co-amoxiclav allergy, use ciprofloxacin + metronidazole; if severe, consider piperacillin-tazobactam.  
  - For IBD: mesalazine (400 mg TDS PO) or steroids (IV hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS) – only after stool cultures negative.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Tranexamic acid: avoid in DIC, thromboembolic disease, or concurrent use of PCC.  
  - Vitamin K IV: risk of anaphylaxis (slow infusion), must be given over 5–10 min.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (20 mg SC OD).  
  - Ciprofloxacin: reduce dose in CKD – adjust per local guideline.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving PCC without checking INR first** – you risk thrombosis. Always confirm INR and discuss with haematology. **Also: giving vitamin K too quickly** – can cause anaphylaxis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Haemorrhagic shock (transfusion requirement >4 units in 24h, ICU escalation).  
  - Bowel perforation (from colonoscopy or active ischaemia).  
  - Aspiration (if NG insertion or massive bleed).  
  - Acute kidney injury (from hypovolaemia or contrast nephropathy).
- **Chronic:**  
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (need aggressive oral iron ± IV iron if unresponsive).  
  - Recurrence (especially in diverticular disease – up to 25% rebleed within 5 years).  
  - Colorectal cancer (if missed index bleed).
sections.Prognosis: - Most lower GI bleeds stop spontaneously (80–85%). Mortality is low (2–5%) but rises to 20% in massive bleeds or elderly with comorbidities. Rebleeding risk is highest in diverticular disease (10–25% at 1 year) and angiodysplasia (up to 50% if not treated).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a PR exam** – you can identify a source and reduce unnecessary scopes.  
- **Document a clear description of the bleeding (volume, colour, frequency)** – this helps the team track improvement.  
- **Check the Oakland score** to identify patients safe for early discharge (score <8) – saves beds.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never attribute fresh PR bleeding to haemorrhoids in an elderly, shocked patient** – think UGIB or diverticular bleed first.  
- **Do not give steroids for suspected IBD until stool cultures are back** – you risk worsening an infection.  
- **Avoid using NSAIDs or aspirin in acute diverticular bleeding** – they increase rebleeding risk.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Any syncope or near-syncope.  
  - New/worsening abdominal pain.  
  - Recurrent fresh bleeding (more than a single episode of streaks).  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP review within 2 weeks – to check FBC, stool cultures if pending, and plan full colonoscopy if not done inpatient (if indicated).  
  - Ensure all patients with diverticular bleeding are advised to increase dietary fibre and avoid NSAIDs.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising trend).  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L.  
  - GCS <15 or evidence of hypoperfusion (cold, mottled, reduced urine output).  
  - Ongoing transfusion requirement >4 units in 24h.  
  - Patient develops peritonism (guarding, rigidity) – suspect perforation.  
  - Any sign of UGIB (haematemesis, melaena) – call GI team for urgent OGD.

rawMarkdown:
# Lower GI Bleed
**One-liner:** Bleeding from colon/rectum/anus, often self-limiting but can cause life-threatening shock in elderly/anticoagulated patients.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Haemorrhagic shock (massive diverticular/angiodysplasia bleed):** A patient with painless, large-volume bright red blood per rectum + tachycardia (HR >100) and hypotension (SBP <90) – do not assume it is trivial.
- **Upper GI bleed presenting as fresh PR blood:** If the patient is shocked but has no melaena/haematemesis, always insert an NG tube (or consider OGD before colonoscopy) – a brisk upper GI bleed can cause bright red PR blood.
- **Mesenteric ischaemia:** Severe abdominal pain *out of proportion* to exam + bloody diarrhoea – lactate >2.5, peritonism – requires urgent CT angiogram / surgical review.
- **Perforated diverticulitis:** Lower quadrant tenderness, guarding, rigid abdomen on exam – do not attribute bleeding to simple diverticulosis; perforation requires emergency surgery.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Bright red blood per rectum (haematochezia), maroon stools, clots, or blood mixed with stool. Tenesmus (rectal urgency) suggests distal source. Nausea/syncope if significant blood loss.
- **Key exam findings:** Pallor, tachycardia, postural hypotension. Abdominal exam: check for localised tenderness (diverticulitis, IBD), guarding/peritonism (mesenteric ischaemia, perforation). PR exam: source (haemorrhoids, fissure, mass), stool colour (bright red vs melaena).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Upper GI bleed** – haematochezia if massive; melaena, NG aspirate positive for blood – differentiate with OGD.
- **Diverticular bleed** – painless, large volume, left-sided – usually self-limiting.
- **Angiodysplasia** – painless, right colon, older patient – may require CT angiography.
- **IBD (UC/Crohn’s)** – bloody diarrhoea, abdominal pain, tenesmus – stool culture before steroids.
- **Colorectal cancer** – chronic, small-volume bleeding, change in bowel habit, weight loss – colonoscopy needed.
- **Haemorrhoids/fissure** – bright red blood on toilet paper, after defaecation – PR exam often diagnostic.
- **Infectious colitis (e.g. Shigella, Campylobacter)** – fever, abdominal cramps, watery diarrhoea ± blood – stool MC&S.
- **Ischaemic colitis** – elderly, cardiovascular disease, acute onset pain, bloody diarrhoea – CT with contrast, do not scope acutely.

**Splitter:** Painless, large-volume bleeding + shock → diverticular/angiodysplasia (not colitis). If pain is present with diarrhoea → IBD or infectious colitis. Melaena/haematemesis → UGIB.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Full vital signs including lying/standing BP. PR exam – document source, stool colour, mass. Place IV access (widebore, ×2 if shocked). NG tube if UGIB suspected (but not always needed).
- **Bloods:** FBC (Hb, platelets), U+E, LFTs, coagulation screen (PT/APTT), group & screen +/- crossmatch if shock. Lactate (metabolic stress). Consider troponin in elderly.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** CT angiography if massive ongoing bleeding (active extravasation) or haemodynamic instability despite resuscitation.  
  - **Second-line:** Colonoscopy after bowel preparation if bleeding slows – also therapeutic (clipping, adrenaline injection, APC).  
- **Special tests:** Capsule endoscopy if colonoscopy negative and ongoing bleed suspected from small bowel.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **Oakland Score (for safe discharge):** Age >60 (1pt), HR >100 (1pt), SBP <100 (1pt), Hb <110 (1pt) – score <8 suggests safe for outpatient management.  
  - **Re-bleeding risk:** Blatchford score (commoner for UGIB but adapted) – not routinely used for LGIB.
- **Never-miss test:** **Coagulation screen (INR, APTT)** – this is the one test you cannot omit; if elevated, reverse immediately. Also **pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age (rules out ectopic/obstetric causes that can mimic).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Resuscitate: IV crystalloid (500–1000 mL stat), blood crossmatch if shock (if Hb <70 g/L, or <80 in cardiac disease).  
  - Reverse anticoagulation: Warfarin → IV vitamin K (10 mg slow IV) + PCC (dose per local policy – [check local policy] for INR-based dose). DOACs → consider idarucizumab (dabigatran) or andexanet alfa (rivaroxaban/apixaban) – use only if major/life-threatening bleed.  
  - If shocked, call senior for possible interventional radiology (embolisation) or urgent OGD to rule out UGIB.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue IV fluids, monitor haemoglobin (repeat fingerprick hourly if ongoing).  
  - Arrange **colonoscopy** (after bowel prep – polyethylene glycol) within 24–48h if stable.  
  - If ongoing bleeding and colonoscopy not possible, discuss with interventional radiology for angiography ± embolisation.  
  - Treat underlying cause: antibiotics if diverticulitis (co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS PO, or ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD + metronidazole 400 mg TDS if penicillin allergy – check local policy).  
  - Iron replacement: oral ferrous fumarate (1–2 tablets BD) if Hb stable but low.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - If haemorrhoids/fissure: high-fibre diet, laxatives (e.g. ispaghula husk), topical lidocaine/prudacaine.  
  - If diverticular disease: increase fibre, avoid NSAIDs.  
  - IBD: refer to gastroenterology for management of colitis.  
  - Cancer: urgent referral to colorectal team (2-week wait).  
  - Iron deficiency workup: repeat FBC at 6 weeks, consider ferritin, colonoscopy if not already done.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV Hartmann’s or 0.9% NaCl at 125 mL/h (adjust for shock).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (40 mg SC OD, or 20 mg if eGFR <30) – hold if actively bleeding, restart once haemostasis achieved.  
  - Diet: NBM if colonoscopy planned; otherwise light diet if stable.  
  - Observations: NEWS 4-hourly (if stable) or hourly if active bleeding.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Tranexamic acid – not routinely recommended for lower GI bleed (evidence lacking), but can consider in significant ongoing bleed (1 g IV over 10 min, then 1 g IV over 8h – but discuss with senior).  
  - Anticoagulation reversal:  
    - Warfarin: Vitamin K 5–10 mg slow IV (check local policy for dose weight-based).  
    - PCC (Beriplex/Octaplex): dose per INR – [check local policy] (e.g. 25–50 U/kg).  
  - Vasopressors: not routine – only if hypotensive despite adequate fluid + blood (discuss with ITU).
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - For diverticulitis: IV antibiotics – if co-amoxiclav allergy, use ciprofloxacin + metronidazole; if severe, consider piperacillin-tazobactam.  
  - For IBD: mesalazine (400 mg TDS PO) or steroids (IV hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS) – only after stool cultures negative.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Tranexamic acid: avoid in DIC, thromboembolic disease, or concurrent use of PCC.  
  - Vitamin K IV: risk of anaphylaxis (slow infusion), must be given over 5–10 min.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (20 mg SC OD).  
  - Ciprofloxacin: reduce dose in CKD – adjust per local guideline.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving PCC without checking INR first** – you risk thrombosis. Always confirm INR and discuss with haematology. **Also: giving vitamin K too quickly** – can cause anaphylaxis.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Haemorrhagic shock (transfusion requirement >4 units in 24h, ICU escalation).  
  - Bowel perforation (from colonoscopy or active ischaemia).  
  - Aspiration (if NG insertion or massive bleed).  
  - Acute kidney injury (from hypovolaemia or contrast nephropathy).
- **Chronic:**  
  - Iron deficiency anaemia (need aggressive oral iron ± IV iron if unresponsive).  
  - Recurrence (especially in diverticular disease – up to 25% rebleed within 5 years).  
  - Colorectal cancer (if missed index bleed).

### Prognosis
- Most lower GI bleeds stop spontaneously (80–85%). Mortality is low (2–5%) but rises to 20% in massive bleeds or elderly with comorbidities. Rebleeding risk is highest in diverticular disease (10–25% at 1 year) and angiodysplasia (up to 50% if not treated).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a PR exam** – you can identify a source and reduce unnecessary scopes.  
- **Document a clear description of the bleeding (volume, colour, frequency)** – this helps the team track improvement.  
- **Check the Oakland score** to identify patients safe for early discharge (score <8) – saves beds.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never attribute fresh PR bleeding to haemorrhoids in an elderly, shocked patient** – think UGIB or diverticular bleed first.  
- **Do not give steroids for suspected IBD until stool cultures are back** – you risk worsening an infection.  
- **Avoid using NSAIDs or aspirin in acute diverticular bleeding** – they increase rebleeding risk.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Any syncope or near-syncope.  
  - New/worsening abdominal pain.  
  - Recurrent fresh bleeding (more than a single episode of streaks).  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP review within 2 weeks – to check FBC, stool cultures if pending, and plan full colonoscopy if not done inpatient (if indicated).  
  - Ensure all patients with diverticular bleeding are advised to increase dietary fibre and avoid NSAIDs.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising trend).  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L.  
  - GCS <15 or evidence of hypoperfusion (cold, mottled, reduced urine output).  
  - Ongoing transfusion requirement >4 units in 24h.  
  - Patient develops peritonism (guarding, rigidity) – suspect perforation.  
  - Any sign of UGIB (haematemesis, melaena) – call GI team for urgent OGD.
